case.question: Beneath which microplate is the Pacific plate subducting in the area of the magnitude 7.5 thrust-fault earthquake that struck off northeastern Honshu on 20 April 2026, and what is the convergence rate range across that boundary?
case.expected_behavior: Pacific plate subducting beneath the Okhotsk microplate; convergence rate 7.9–9.2 cm per year. Source: '2026 Sanriku earthquake' (Tectonic setting).

agent_run.question: Beneath which microplate is the Pacific plate subducting in the area of the magnitude 7.5 thrust-fault earthquake that struck off northeastern Honshu on 20 April 2026, and what is the convergence rate range across that boundary?
agent_run.answer:
The Wikipedia articles provide a clear and complete answer.

The magnitude 7.5 thrust-fault earthquake that struck off the **Sanriku Coast** of northeastern Honshu on 20 April 2026 occurred along the **Japan Trench**, where the **Pacific plate** is subducting beneath the **Okhotsk microplate** [2026 Sanriku earthquake][Japan Trench]. The Okhotsk microplate is a microplate formerly considered part of the North American plate [Japan Trench].

The convergence rate across this Pacific–Okhotsk boundary is in the range of **7.9 to 9.2 cm per year** [2026 Sanriku earthquake][Japan Trench].

Sources:
- 2026 Sanriku earthquake
- Japan Trench

case.question: At the convergent boundary marked by the Sunda Trench off the southern coast of Java — site of the 6 February 2026 Mw 5.8 thrust-motion earthquake — which plate subducts beneath which, and what is the convergence rate at the western and eastern parts of the trench?
case.expected_behavior: The Australian Plate subducts northward beneath the Sunda Plate. Convergence rate is about 6 cm/yr in the western part and 4.9 cm/yr in the eastern part. The article also notes the Sunda subduction zone is capable of generating megathrust earthquakes up to magnitude 8.7. Source: '2026 Pacitan earthquake' (Tectonic setting).

agent_run.question: At the convergent boundary marked by the Sunda Trench off the southern coast of Java — site of the 6 February 2026 Mw 5.8 thrust-motion earthquake — which plate subducts beneath which, and what is the convergence rate at the western and eastern parts of the trench?
agent_run.answer:
I now have all the information needed. Here is the complete answer:

---

## Sunda Trench: Subducting Plates and Convergence Rates

**Which plate subducts beneath which:**
The Sunda Trench marks an active convergent boundary between the **Australian Plate** (to the south) and the **Sunda Plate** (to the north). The northward-moving Australian Plate subducts beneath the Sunda Plate [2026 Pacitan earthquake]. This subduction involves relatively old oceanic crust and is accompanied by a deepening of the Benioff Zone (steepening dip angle) [2026 Pacitan earthquake].

**Convergence rates:**
- **Western part** of the trench (Java section): approximately **6 cm (2.4 in) per year**
- **Eastern part** of the trench (Java section, toward Bali): approximately **4.9 cm (1.9 in) per year**

[2026 Pacitan earthquake]

**About the earthquake itself:**
The 6 February 2026 event (Mw 5.8 per USGS, though initially recorded higher by Indonesia's BMKG) struck Pacitan Regency, East Java, with its epicenter ~89 km southeast of Pacitan at a depth of ~40–58 km. Source mechanism analysis confirmed **thrust motion**, consistent with the compressional nature of this subduction zone [2026 Pacitan earthquake].

Sources:
- 2026 Pacitan earthquake

arguments.query: Sunda Trench subduction plate convergence rate
Sunda Trench Java Trench tectonic plates subduction

results.closed_book_answer:
The 6 February 2026 earthquake you mention falls after my knowledge cutoff (early 2025), so I cannot confirm details about that specific event.

However, I can answer the tectonic setting question from established knowledge:

## Sunda Trench Subduction Zone

**Which plate subducts beneath which:**
The **Indo-Australian Plate** (or more precisely the Indian/Australian plate) subducts beneath the **Eurasian Plate** (Sunda microplate/block) along the Sunda Trench (also called the Java Trench).

## Convergence Rates

The convergence rate varies significantly along the trench:

- **Western part (off Sumatra):** approximately **52–60 mm/yr**, with a significant oblique component — this obliquity is partly accommodated by the Sumatran Fault (strike-slip) running onshore.
- **Eastern part (off Java):** approximately **60–70 mm/yr**, with convergence being more **orthogonal** (perpendicular) to the trench compared to the Sumatra segment.

The direction of convergence rotates from more northeastward motion in the west to more northward/northwestward motion in the east, and the rate generally increases eastward along the Java segment.

These figures come from geodetic and plate motion model studies (e.g., NUVEL-1A, MORVEL).
results.reference_answer: At the Sunda Trench off southern Java, the northward-moving Australian Plate subducts beneath the Sunda Plate. Convergence of relatively old oceanic crust occurs at a rate of 6 cm (2.4 in) per year in the western part and 4.9 cm (1.9 in) per year in the eastern part of the trench [2026 Pacitan earthquake].

closed_book_judge.comment: System refused to look up the event but then provided convergence rates (52-70 mm/yr) that contradict the Wikipedia figures (6 cm/yr west, 4.9 cm/yr east) without any citations or article fetches.

case.question: The Molucca Sea region — site of a Mw 7.3–7.4 reverse-faulting earthquake on 2 April 2026 — is described as the only global example of which type of tectonic setting, and which two microplates subsume the Molucca Sea plate?
case.expected_behavior: Only global example of an active arc–arc collision consuming an oceanic basin via subduction in two directions (the 'Molucca Sea Collision Zone'). The Halmahera plate and the Sangihe plate subsume the Molucca Sea plate. Source: '2026 North Maluku earthquake' (Tectonic setting).

agent_run.question: The Molucca Sea region — site of a Mw 7.3–7.4 reverse-faulting earthquake on 2 April 2026 — is described as the only global example of which type of tectonic setting, and which two microplates subsume the Molucca Sea plate?
agent_run.answer:
Both articles provide clear, complementary answers. Here is the full picture:

The Molucca Sea region is described as **the only global example of an active arc-arc collision** — specifically, one that consumes an oceanic basin via subduction in *two opposite directions* (a process known as **divergent double subduction, or DDS**) [2026 North Maluku earthquake][Molucca Sea plate]. The Molucca Sea plate is the only known example of this divergent double subduction, where a single oceanic plate is subducted on both of its sides simultaneously [Molucca Sea plate].

The Molucca Sea plate is subsumed by two tectonic microplates: the **Halmahera plate** (situated along its eastern boundary, moving northwest) and the **Sangihe plate** (situated along its western boundary, moving southeast) [2026 North Maluku earthquake][Molucca Sea plate].

Sources:
- 2026 North Maluku earthquake
- Molucca Sea plate

results.closed_book_answer:
I need to be transparent here: the earthquake you describe — a Mw 7.3–7.4 reverse-faulting event on 2 April 2026 in the Molucca Sea — occurred after my knowledge cutoff, so I cannot confirm details about that specific event.

However, I can answer the tectonic background question from my training data:

**Tectonic setting:** The Molucca Sea is classically described as the only known example of a **double subduction zone** (or bivergent/double-sided subduction), where the Molucca Sea plate is being simultaneously subducted in **opposite directions** — westward beneath the Sangihe Arc and eastward beneath the Halmahera Arc. This makes it a unique "tectonic vise" or **double-arc collision** setting.

**The two microplates:** The Molucca Sea plate is being consumed by (and caught between) the **Sangihe microplate** (to the west) and the **Halmahera microplate** (to the east).
results.reference_answer: The Molucca Sea region is described as the only global example of an active arc-arc collision consuming an oceanic basin via subduction in two directions. The Molucca Sea plate is subsumed by two microplates: the Halmahera plate and the Sangihe plate. This complex zone is known as the Molucca Sea Collision Zone [2026 North Maluku earthquake].

closed_book_judge.comment: Substantively correct on tectonic setting and microplates, but no search was performed and no citations or Sources list provided.

case.question: According to the Wikipedia article describing the 3 April 2026 Mw 5.8 deep-focus earthquake near Jorm, Afghanistan, the very deep hypocenters (170–280 km) in the Hindu Kush region are sourced from within which tectonic plate as it descends, and what process leads to stress accommodation that produces the earthquakes?

agent_run.question: According to the Wikipedia article describing the 3 April 2026 Mw 5.8 deep-focus earthquake near Jorm, Afghanistan, the very deep hypocenters (170–280 km) in the Hindu Kush region are sourced from within which tectonic plate as it descends, and what process leads to stress accommodation that produces the earthquakes?
agent_run.answer:
According to the Wikipedia article on the 2026 Jorm earthquake, the very deep hypocenters (170–280 km) in the Hindu Kush region are sourced from **within the Indian plate** as it dives beneath the Hindu Kush [2026 Jorm earthquake]. As the tectonic slab of the Indian plate descends at a near-vertical angle into the mantle, it stretches and begins to "tear," eventually leading to a **slab detachment**. This action results in **stress accommodation along faults**, which produces earthquakes when those faults rupture [2026 Jorm earthquake].

Sources:
- 2026 Jorm earthquake

case.expected_behavior: The plate is the Indian Plate. From the Indian Plate Wikipedia article: it is converging with the Eurasian Plate at the Himalayas, at approximately 5 cm/year (the article's stated rate; accept 4–5 cm/year as long as the answer cites the Indian Plate article). The agent must fetch the 'Indian Plate' article to answer correctly. Sources: '2026 Jorm earthquake' (to identify the plate from context) and 'Indian Plate' (for the convergence rate).

agent_run.question: The 3 April 2026 Mw 5.8 deep-focus earthquake near Jorm in northeastern Afghanistan is sourced from within a tectonic plate that is descending into the mantle beneath the Hindu Kush. According to the Wikipedia article on that plate itself (NOT the earthquake article), with which other major tectonic plate is it converging at the Himalayas, and at approximately what convergence rate?
agent_run.answer:
I now have everything I need from both articles. Here is the answer:

The earthquake is sourced from within the **Indian Plate**, which is descending (subducting) at a near-vertical angle into the mantle beneath the Hindu Kush [2026 Jorm earthquake].

According to the Wikipedia article on the **Indian Plate** itself, it is converging with the **Eurasian Plate** at the Himalayas. Specifically, the Indian Plate is currently moving north-east at **5 cm (2.0 in) per year**, while the Eurasian Plate is moving north at only **2 cm (0.79 in) per year**, giving a relative convergence on the order of approximately **~5 cm per year** (with the Eurasian plate deforming and the Indian plate compressing at a rate of 4 mm/year) [Indian plate]. The northerly boundary of the Indian Plate is described as a convergent boundary with the Eurasian Plate forming the Himalaya and Hindu Kush mountains, along the **Main Himalayan Thrust** [Indian plate].

Sources:
- 2026 Jorm earthquake
- Indian plate

case.question: On 19 January 2026 at 11:21:12 PST, a Mw 5.6 earthquake struck near Barishal, in the far north of which Pakistani administrative territory? Which valley in Hunza district was severely affected, and how many people were killed?
case.expected_behavior: Barishal is in Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan. The Chapursan valley in Hunza district was severely affected. Two people were killed and five others were injured. Source: '2026 Gilgit-Baltistan earthquake'.

agent_run.question: On 19 January 2026 at 11:21:12 PST, a Mw 5.6 earthquake struck near Barishal, in the far north of which Pakistani administrative territory? Which valley in Hunza district was severely affected, and how many people were killed?
agent_run.answer:
Here is a full answer based on the Wikipedia article:

The **Mw 5.6 earthquake on 19 January 2026 at 11:21:12 PST** struck near Barishal, in the far north of **Gilgit-Baltistan**, a Pakistani administrative territory near the Afghanistan–China border and the Durand Line [2026 Gilgit-Baltistan earthquake].

The valley of **Chapursan**, in **Hunza district**, was severely affected — the access road was blocked at multiple locations, over 600 houses were affected (with 300 damaged beyond repair), large cracks developed across parts of the valley, and roads and water channels were damaged [2026 Gilgit-Baltistan earthquake].

**Two people were killed** (and five others were injured) as a result of the earthquake [2026 Gilgit-Baltistan earthquake].

Sources:
- 2026 Gilgit-Baltistan earthquake

case.question: On 10 June 1836, an estimated magnitude 6.8 earthquake occurred along a strike-slip fault zone in what was then Mexican-administered Alta California, causing damage at Mission San Jose and Mission Santa Clara de Asís. Along which fault zone did it occur, and what is the regional plate-boundary type the fault is part of?
case.expected_behavior: Hayward Fault Zone, part of the San Andreas Fault system — a major transform boundary between the Pacific Plate and the North American Plate. Earthquakes along this system are typically strike-slip. Source: '1836 Hayward earthquake'.

agent_run.question: On 10 June 1836, an estimated magnitude 6.8 earthquake occurred along a strike-slip fault zone in what was then Mexican-administered Alta California, causing damage at Mission San Jose and Mission Santa Clara de Asís. Along which fault zone did it occur, and what is the regional plate-boundary type the fault is part of?
agent_run.answer:
I now have everything needed to answer the question fully.

The June 10, 1836 earthquake occurred along the **Hayward Fault Zone** [1836 Hayward earthquake]. The Hayward Fault Zone is a right-lateral strike-slip fault zone running roughly 74 miles (119 km) along the western base of the hills on the east side of San Francisco Bay, passing through areas such as Fremont (where Mission San José is located) [Hayward Fault Zone].

In terms of the broader regional tectonics, the Hayward Fault Zone is part of the **San Andreas Fault system**, which forms a **transform plate boundary** between the Pacific Plate and the North American Plate [Hayward Fault Zone][1836 Hayward earthquake]. While the San Andreas Fault is the principal transform boundary between the two plates, the Hayward Fault is one of the secondary faults in this diffuse transform zone that together accommodate the overall relative displacement between the Pacific and North American plates [Hayward Fault Zone].

Sources:
- 1836 Hayward earthquake
- Hayward Fault Zone

results.reference_answer: The 1836 earthquake occurred along the Hayward Fault Zone, which is part of the San Andreas Fault system—a major transform plate boundary between the Pacific Plate and the North American Plate [1836 Hayward earthquake].

case.question: Among the post-September-2025 Wikipedia-documented earthquakes — specifically the 2 April 2026 Molucca Sea event (BMKG Mw 7.3 / USGS Mww 7.4) and the 20 April 2026 off-Honshu event along the Japan Trench (Mw 7.4–7.5) — which one had the higher moment magnitude? Then compare the larger of those two 2026 events to the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake along the same Japan-Trench plate boundary: by how many Mw units does the 2011 event exceed it, and given that an increase of 1.0 in Mw corresponds to roughly 31.6× more seismic-moment energy released, what is the approximate energy ratio?
case.expected_behavior: The 20 April 2026 off-Honshu event was the larger of the two 2026 events (Mw 7.4–7.5 vs Mw 7.3–7.4). The 2011 Tōhoku earthquake was Mw 9.0–9.1, roughly 1.5–1.7 Mw units larger. Energy ratio: 31.6^1.5 ≈ 178× to 31.6^1.7 ≈ 250× more energy released. The agent must fetch the 2011 Tōhoku article to ground the comparison. Sources: '2026 North Maluku earthquake', '2026 Sanriku earthquake', and '2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami'.

agent_run.question: Among the post-September-2025 Wikipedia-documented earthquakes — specifically the 2 April 2026 Molucca Sea event (BMKG Mw 7.3 / USGS Mww 7.4) and the 20 April 2026 off-Honshu event along the Japan Trench (Mw 7.4–7.5) — which one had the higher moment magnitude? Then compare the larger of those two 2026 events to the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake along the same Japan-Trench plate boundary: by how many Mw units does the 2011 event exceed it, and given that an increase of 1.0 in Mw corresponds to roughly 31.6× more seismic-moment energy released, what is the approximate energy ratio?
agent_run.answer:
I now have all the information I need from Wikipedia. Here is the full, grounded answer:

---

## Comparing the Two 2026 Earthquakes

**2 April 2026 — 2026 North Maluku earthquake (Molucca Sea):**
BMKG reported **Mw 7.3**; USGS reported **Mww 7.4** [2026 North Maluku earthquake].

**20 April 2026 — 2026 Sanriku earthquake (off Honshu, Japan Trench):**
USGS reported **Mw 7.4**; the Global Centroid Moment Tensor (GCMT) assigned **Mw 7.5**; JMA's final figure was **MJMA 7.5** [2026 Sanriku earthquake].

**Which had the higher moment magnitude?** The **2026 Sanriku earthquake** had the higher moment magnitude. Even at its lower-bound estimate (Mw 7.4, matching the USGS Molucca Sea figure), the GCMT's authoritative centroid-moment-tensor solution placed it at **Mw 7.5**, making it the larger event [2026 Sanriku earthquake][2026 North Maluku earthquake].

---

## Comparing the Sanriku Event to the 2011 Tōhoku Earthquake

**2011 Tōhoku earthquake:** Mw **9.1** (per the USGS final figure, upgraded on 11 July 2016), making it the most powerful earthquake ever recorded in Japan and the fourth most powerful recorded worldwide since 1900 [2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami].

**Magnitude difference:**
Using the Sanriku event's best-estimate magnitude of **Mw 7.5** (GCMT):

> 9.1 − 7.5 = **1.6 Mw units**

---

## Energy Ratio

The Mw scale is logarithmic: each **1.0 unit increase ≈ 31.6× more seismic-moment energy**. For a difference of **1.6 units**, the energy ratio is:

> 31.6^1.6 ≈ **~178×**

More precisely: 31.6^1.6 = e^(1.6 × ln 31.6) = e^(1.6 × 3.454) = e^5.526 ≈ **~250×**

Let's be careful here using the standard formula. Each 1.0 Mw = 31.623× in seismic moment energy, so:

- 31.623^1.6 = (10^1.5)^1.6 = 10^(1.5 × 1.6) = 10^2.4 ≈ **~251×**

So the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake released approximately **~250 times** more seismic-moment energy than the 2026 Sanriku earthquake.

---

## Summary Table

| Event | Magnitude | Plate Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| 2026 North Maluku (Molucca Sea) | Mw 7.3–7.4 | Arc-arc collision (Molucca Sea) |
| **2026 Sanriku (off Honshu)** | **Mw 7.4–7.5** | Japan Trench |
| 2011 Tōhoku | **Mw 9.1** | Japan Trench |

- **Larger 2026 event:** 2026 Sanriku earthquake (Mw 7.5 per GCMT)
- **Tōhoku exceeds it by:** 1.6 Mw units
- **Approximate energy ratio (Tōhoku ÷ Sanriku):** 10^(1.5 × 1.6) = 10^2.4 ≈ **~251×**

---

Sources:
- 2026 North Maluku earthquake
- 2026 Sanriku earthquake
- 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami

The 20 April 2026 Sanriku earthquake, with Mw 7.4–7.5 (GCMT assigned Mw 7.5) [2026 Sanriku earthquake], had a higher moment magnitude than the 2 April 2026 Molucca Sea event, which measured BMKG Mw 7.3 / USGS Mww 7.4 [2026 North Maluku earthquake].

Using the given conversion (1.0 Mw ≈ 31.6× more seismic-moment energy), an increase of ~1.6 Mw corresponds to roughly 31.6^1.6 ≈ 178× more energy. So the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake released on the order of ~180 times more seismic-moment energy than the April 2026 Sanriku event.

case.question: Across four of the post-September-2025 Wikipedia-documented earthquakes — the 19 January 2026 Mw 5.6 event near Barishal in Pakistan's far north; the 6 February 2026 Mw 5.8 event offshore East Java; the 3 April 2026 Mw 5.8 event south of Jorm, Afghanistan; and the 20 April 2026 Mw 7.5 event off northeastern Honshu — which two had the SAME moment magnitude, and which one of those four had the deepest reported hypocenter (with what depth)?
case.expected_behavior: The 6 February 2026 East Java event (USGS Mw 5.8) and the 3 April 2026 Jorm event (Mw 5.8) shared the same magnitude. The Jorm event had the deepest hypocenter at 186.4 km. (The Honshu event was 35 km, the East Java event 40 km USGS / 58 km BMKG, the Gilgit-Baltistan event was Mw 5.6.) Sources: all four named earthquake articles.

agent_run.question: Across four of the post-September-2025 Wikipedia-documented earthquakes — the 19 January 2026 Mw 5.6 event near Barishal in Pakistan's far north; the 6 February 2026 Mw 5.8 event offshore East Java; the 3 April 2026 Mw 5.8 event south of Jorm, Afghanistan; and the 20 April 2026 Mw 7.5 event off northeastern Honshu — which two had the SAME moment magnitude, and which one of those four had the deepest reported hypocenter (with what depth)?
agent_run.answer:
I now have all the information needed. Here is a thorough, sourced answer:

---

**Important caveat up front:** The question contains a factual error about one of the four events. The 19 January 2026 Barishal event is **not** in "Pakistan's far north" in any geographically ambiguous way — it *is* in Gilgit-Baltistan — but more critically, the question mis-describes one magnitude: the 20 April 2026 Sanriku earthquake off northeastern Honshu is recorded by USGS at **Mw 7.4** and by GCMT at **Mw 7.5**, *not* a clean Mw 7.5 [2026 Sanriku earthquake]. With that noted, here are the verified facts:

| Event | Date | Mw | Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Barishal, Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan | 19 Jan 2026 | **5.6** | not stated in article |
| Pacitan, offshore East Java | 6 Feb 2026 | **5.8** | 40 km (per USGS) |
| South of Jorm, Afghanistan (Hindu Kush) | 3 Apr 2026 | **5.8** | **186.4 km** |
| Sanriku Coast, off NE Honshu, Japan | 20 Apr 2026 | **7.4–7.5** | 35 km |

**The two events with the same moment magnitude (Mw 5.8)** are the **6 February 2026 Pacitan earthquake** (offshore East Java) and the **3 April 2026 Jorm earthquake** (Hindu Kush, Afghanistan) [2026 Pacitan earthquake][2026 Jorm earthquake].

**Of those four earthquakes, the deepest hypocenter by far belongs to the 3 April 2026 Jorm, Afghanistan earthquake**, at a reported depth of **186.4 km (116 mi)** [2026 Jorm earthquake]. This makes geological sense, as Hindu Kush earthquakes are characteristically deep intermediate-focus events sourced from within the Indian plate as it subducts steeply beneath the region [2026 Jorm earthquake]. By comparison, the Pacitan event was at ~40 km [2026 Pacitan earthquake] and the Sanriku event at ~35 km [2026 Sanriku earthquake].

---

Sources:
- 2026 Gilgit-Baltistan earthquake
- 2026 Pacitan earthquake
- 2026 Jorm earthquake
- 2026 Sanriku earthquake
